content.text: Program Increment (PI) Planning is the signature ceremony of the Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe), bringing all teams on an Agile Release Train together for a 2-day event to plan, coordinate, and commit to their objectives for the next 8-12 weeks. For GenAI initiatives, PI Planning requires deliberate adaptation: the nature of AI features, model evaluation cycles, and data pipeline dependencies introduces coordination challenges that standard PI Planning agendas do not explicitly address.
What It Is

PI Planning is a face-to-face (or virtual) event held at the start of each Program Increment, typically every 8-12 weeks. All teams on the Agile Release Train (ART) attend simultaneously, enabling direct cross-team dependency resolution that asynchronous processes cannot achieve efficiently. The event produces PI Objectives (committed and uncommitted goals for the increment), a program board showing features and cross-team dependencies, a ROAM (Resolved, Owned, Accepted, Mitigated) risk register, and a team confidence vote that provides a collective signal on whether the plan is achievable.

For GenAI initiatives, PI Planning must incorporate AI-specific workstreams: model evaluation milestones, training data readiness, GPU capacity planning, and spike allocation for research activities whose outcomes are unknown in advance. Without explicit planning for these, AI teams either over-commit to feature delivery at the expense of model quality work, or under-commit because they cannot translate experimental work into meaningful sprint commitments.
Why It Matters

GenAI programs frequently fail at scale because of coordination failures between model teams, data teams, product teams, and infrastructure teams. The model team's next training run depends on a dataset that the data team is still building. The product team is building features against a model API that the ML team is planning to change. The infrastructure team has allocated GPU capacity that assumes 2x daily training runs when the actual need is 5x. PI Planning surfaces all of these conflicts in a 2-day event rather than discovering them 4 weeks into the increment when the cost of replanning is much higher.

For GenAI-specific work, PI Planning also provides the moment where the sponsor and business stakeholders see the model quality objectives alongside the product feature objectives — creating visibility into the AI quality investment that is often missing when sprint reviews focus only on demonstrable software features.
Benefits
Cross-team dependency resolution: face-to-face or video dependency resolution is 10x faster than asynchronous Jira-based coordination.
Committed model quality objectives: PI Objectives that include model evaluation metrics make quality improvement a visible commitment, not a background activity.
GPU capacity planning: explicit infrastructure capacity planning prevents the common failure where training jobs queue behind each other with no coordination.
Spike allocation: a dedicated discussion of research spike capacity ensures that experimentation is protected from feature delivery pressure.
Data dependency visibility: a program board that shows data pipeline deliverables alongside feature deliverables prevents invisible data blocking.

Stakeholder alignment: business stakeholders who attend the PI Planning event understand the nature of GenAI work — including model retraining cycles and evaluation requirements — reducing unrealistic expectations.
Trade-offs
Time investment: 2 days every 8-12 weeks is significant; for small teams this can feel disproportionate.

Uncertainty in AI planning: model training outcomes are genuinely uncertain — committing to specific model quality targets in PI Objectives can be stressful for ML teams if the training dynamics are not well understood.
Distributed team challenges: PI Planning is most effective face-to-face; geographically distributed GenAI teams (common in AI talent acquisition) face facilitation challenges in virtual PI Planning.
Over-planning risk: prescriptive sprint-level planning in PI Planning can conflict with the exploration nature of AI development where the path to a model quality target is not known in advance.
How It Works
Pre-PI Planning Preparation

Effective PI Planning requires 2-3 weeks of preparation. Product Managers refine the feature backlog for the upcoming PI, including both user-facing features and model quality improvements. The System Architect prepares an architecture briefing covering proposed model serving changes, data platform updates, and any significant technical decisions that will affect team planning. The ML Lead prepares a 'model readiness assessment' — a summary of current model quality metrics, known training data issues, and the evaluation framework for the PI. Infrastructure confirms GPU quota and availability for training runs during the PI.
Day 1: Context and Team Breakouts

Day 1 opens with business context: the VP or sponsor presents portfolio priorities, the evaluation targets the model must reach by the end of the PI, and any AI risk or regulatory items that constrain what the teams can deliver. The System Architect then presents the architecture briefing. A feature slicing workshop follows where Product Managers, architects, and team representatives break planned features into their component workstreams — model work, data work, product/integration work, and infrastructure work. This slicing is essential for GenAI planning because a single 'feature' from the product backlog often requires parallel work across three or four teams.

Teams then spend 3 hours in parallel breakouts drafting their sprint plans and PI Objectives. The ML team's planning must include: evaluation milestones (which sprint will the model be trained and evaluated on the acceptance test set?), spike allocation (what percentage of capacity is reserved for experiments?), and model versioning checkpoints (which sprints will produce candidate model versions for review?). The data team's planning must show which data pipeline deliverables are needed by the ML team and when.
Day 2: Finalize and Commit

Day 2 opens with teams adjusting their plans based on Day 1 feedback and dependency conflicts identified in the first draft review. The final program board is reviewed with all cross-team dependencies mapped. Each team presents their PI Objectives in 5 minutes: committed objectives (the team is confident these will be achieved), uncommitted objectives (stretch goals the team will attempt if capacity allows), and confidence votes per objective. The ROAM session processes identified risks: which are Resolved (addressed before the PI starts), Owned (one person takes accountability for monitoring), Accepted (the team accepts the risk with no active mitigation), and Mitigated (a mitigation action is in place). The PI concludes with a team confidence vote and executive review.
GenAI-Specific PI Artifacts

Beyond the standard SAFe PI artefacts, GenAI programs should produce: a model evaluation milestone calendar (which sprint delivers each evaluation checkpoint and what the pass/fail criteria are); a GPU capacity plan (aggregate training compute needed by sprint, compared to available quota); a data dependency map (which ML workstream depends on which data pipeline deliverable, with dates); a spike capacity register (named spike topics, allocated capacity per sprint, expected outputs); and an AI risk ROAM board with GenAI-specific risk categories (model drift, data quality, evaluation failure, regulatory change).
Feature Slicing for AI

Feature slicing for AI is the practice of decomposing a product feature into its AI-specific and non-AI components before PI Planning so that each component can be planned independently. A feature like 'smart email draft suggestions' decomposes into: data (email corpus cleaning, labelling schema definition), model (intent classification fine-tuning, suggestion generation evaluation), infrastructure (model serving for email client integration), and product (email client UI integration, feedback collection). Each component is a separate feature on the relevant team's backlog with its own acceptance criteria and delivery sprint. Without this decomposition, AI features are treated as monolithic software tasks and the cross-team dependencies remain invisible.
Architecture

attrs.alt: PI Planning 2-day agenda for GenAI initiatives showing Day 1 context and breakouts, Day 2 finalise and commit, with GenAI-specific artefacts

content.text: When to Use It
Every 8-12 weeks for any GenAI program running on SAFe with an Agile Release Train.
Before any PI that includes a major model version upgrade — the coordination complexity of a model transition requires face-to-face planning.
When a new GenAI team joins the ART — the PI Planning event is the best onboarding mechanism for establishing working relationships and understanding cross-team dependencies.
When a previous PI had significant mid-increment dependency failures — a focused PI Planning with explicit dependency mapping reduces recurrence.
How to Implement
Assign a PI Planning facilitator (RTE) at least 3 weeks before the event — logistics, venue/virtual setup, and agenda coordination take significant time.
Brief all Product Managers on feature slicing for AI before their backlog refinement sessions — unsliced AI features are the main cause of poor PI plans.
Confirm GPU capacity and data platform availability with infrastructure before the architecture briefing — unconfirmed capacity creates false commitments.
Create a PI Planning template for ML team objectives that explicitly includes evaluation milestones and spike capacity rows — do not leave these to team discretion.
Schedule a 30-minute 'model readiness review' as a mandatory pre-PI artifact — the ML lead presents current model quality and training data status to all teams before planning begins.
Configure the program board to show data pipeline milestones alongside feature milestones — invisible data dependencies are the most common cross-team failure in GenAI PI Planning.
Hold a PI retrospective 2 days after the event while details are fresh — capture what to do differently in the next PI Planning session.
Examples
ML Team PI Objectives Template

# ML Team PI Objectives — PI 4 (2026-Q3)
# ART: Customer Intent Classifier | Team: ML Core

## Committed Objectives
| # | Objective                                        | Metric / Acceptance Criteria              | Confidence |
|---|--------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|------------|
| 1 | Reduce hallucination rate to <3% on eval set v7  | Auto-eval on 1000-query set ≥97% correct  | 8/10       |
| 2 | Complete RLHF cycle on feedback batch Jul-2026   | Preference model trained + eval pass      | 7/10       |
| 3 | Deliver v2.3 model to staging by Sprint 4        | Model card + eval results in registry     | 9/10       |

## Uncommitted Objectives (stretch)
| # | Objective                                        | Metric / Acceptance Criteria              | Confidence |
|---|--------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|------------|
| U1| Evaluate GPT-4o-mini as alternative base model   | Comparative eval report vs current base   | 6/10       |
| U2| Implement chain-of-thought prompting for complex | A/B test: ≥5pp accuracy lift on complex  | 5/10       |

## Spike Allocation (20% = 2 sprint-days per sprint)
| Sprint | Spike Topic                          | Expected Output             |
|--------|--------------------------------------|-----------------------------|
| S1     | Evaluate embedding model alternatives| Benchmark comparison report  |
| S2     | Red-team adversarial prompt set      | 200-item adversarial set     |
| S3     | Hallucination detection classifier   | PoC accuracy report          |
| S4     | Quantization impact on accuracy      | Accuracy vs latency tradeoff |
| S5     | (buffer for unexpected issues)       | —                            |

## Dependencies (from ML team)
- Data team: annotated Jul-2026 feedback batch by Sprint 1 Day 5
- Infra team: 8xA100 GPU cluster available from Sprint 1
- Platform team: evaluation pipeline v2 deployed before Sprint 2

## Risks
| ID  | Description                             | ROAM    | Owner     |
|-----|----------------------------------------|---------|-----------|
| R-1 | Annotation quality in Jul batch unclear | Owned   | Data Lead |
| R-2 | GPU cluster expansion delayed           | Mitigated | Infra (workaround: shared cloud) |
Feature slicing for AI — Python planning utility

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum

class Workstream(Enum):
    DATA = "data"
    MODEL = "model"
    INFRA = "infra"
    PRODUCT = "product"

@dataclass
class AIFeatureComponent:
    title: str
    workstream: Workstream
    owner_team: str
    sprint_target: int
    story_points: int
    depends_on: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    acceptance_criteria: str = ""

@dataclass
class AIFeature:
    name: str
    description: str
    components: list[AIFeatureComponent] = field(default_factory=list)

    def add_component(self, component: AIFeatureComponent):
        self.components.append(component)

    def dependency_summary(self) -> list[str]:
        deps = []
        for c in self.components:
            for d in c.depends_on:
                deps.append(f"{c.owner_team} (Sprint {c.sprint_target}) depends on: {d}")
        return deps

# Example: slice "Smart email draft suggestions" into components
feature = AIFeature(
    name="Smart Email Draft Suggestions",
    description="AI generates draft email responses for customer service agents"
)

feature.add_component(AIFeatureComponent(
    title="Email corpus labelling schema",
    workstream=Workstream.DATA,
    owner_team="Data Team",
    sprint_target=1,
    story_points=5,
    acceptance_criteria="Labelling guide complete, 100 emails annotated as seed set"
))
feature.add_component(AIFeatureComponent(
    title="Intent classification fine-tuning",
    workstream=Workstream.MODEL,
    owner_team="ML Team",
    sprint_target=3,
    story_points=8,
    depends_on=["Data Team: labelled corpus (500+ examples) by Sprint 2"],
    acceptance_criteria="F1 ≥0.88 on intent classification eval set"
))
feature.add_component(AIFeatureComponent(
    title="Draft generation evaluation framework",
    workstream=Workstream.MODEL,
    owner_team="ML Team",
    sprint_target=4,
    story_points=5,
    depends_on=["Product Team: accepted draft quality rubric by Sprint 2"],
    acceptance_criteria="Automated eval: ROUGE-L ≥0.6, human eval: ≥80% acceptable"
))
feature.add_component(AIFeatureComponent(
    title="Email client API integration",
    workstream=Workstream.PRODUCT,
    owner_team="Product Team",
    sprint_target=5,
    story_points=8,
    depends_on=["ML Team: model endpoint stable (v2.3) by Sprint 4"],
    acceptance_criteria="Suggestions displayed in UI; agent can accept/edit/reject"
))

print(f"Feature: {feature.name}")
print(f"Components: {len(feature.components)}")
print("\nDependencies:")
for dep in feature.dependency_summary():
    print(f"  - {dep}")
print("\nComponent summary:")
for c in feature.components:
    print(f"  [{c.workstream.value.upper()}] S{c.sprint_target}: {c.title} ({c.story_points}pts) — {c.owner_team}")
Real-World Example

A digital health company running a GenAI clinical note generation feature experienced chronic mid-sprint rework during their first three PIs because the ML team's model training depended on annotated clinical text that the data team consistently delivered 1-2 sprints later than expected. In PI 4, the RTE introduced a mandatory feature slicing workshop before team breakouts: each planned feature was explicitly decomposed into data, model, product, and infrastructure components, with data components identified as planning predecessors (their completion was a dependency for model components). The program board for PI 4 had explicit data pipeline milestones tracked alongside product features for the first time. Cross-team dependency calls between the data and ML teams shifted from reactive (blocked Slack messages at sprint mid-point) to proactive (weekly 15-minute data readiness check built into the sprint ceremony schedule). Mid-PI rework dropped by 60% in PI 4 compared to PI 3.
Common Mistakes
Treating model training as a sprint commitment rather than a milestone with uncertainty — training outcomes are not deterministic; frame model objectives as outcome targets, not delivery promises.
Not allocating spike capacity explicitly — teams that do not explicitly protect spike time find it consumed by feature delivery within 2-3 sprints.
Treating data pipeline deliverables as invisible — data team deliverables that are prerequisites for model training must appear on the program board as explicit milestones.
Skipping the model readiness pre-assessment — entering PI Planning without knowing current model quality and training data status leads to uninformed commitments.
Over-committing to model quality improvements — ML teams should use confidence levels honestly; a 5/10 confidence objective should be uncommitted.
Virtual PI Planning with poor facilitation tools — dependency mapping, which is the most value-generating activity in PI Planning, falls apart in unstructured virtual settings.
Best Practices
Conduct a feature slicing workshop before team breakouts — without AI-specific decomposition, dependencies remain invisible.
Include GPU capacity plan and model evaluation milestone calendar as mandatory PI Planning outputs alongside the standard SAFe artefacts.
Protect spike allocation at 15-20% of team capacity in the PI plan — negotiate this as a first-class planning element with the product sponsor before PI Planning begins.
Use ROAM explicitly for GenAI risks: data quality uncertainty, compute availability, evaluation criterion ambiguity, and regulatory change are common AI-specific risks for the ROAM board.
Include a confidence vote per PI Objective — for model quality objectives, confidence levels below 7/10 should prompt the team to make those objectives uncommitted rather than committed.
Debrief within 2 days of PI Planning while memory is fresh — the most valuable retrospective data about what to improve in the next PI comes immediately after the event.
Performance & Scalability

For large ARTs with 5+ teams and multiple model families, PI Planning duration increases and dependency complexity grows non-linearly. Address this by: running a separate 'Technical Enabler' planning session for infrastructure and platform dependencies before main PI Planning; using a digital dependency board (Miro, Microsoft Whiteboard) that all teams can update simultaneously rather than a physical board that one person manages; and assigning a dedicated dependency manager role for the PI Planning event — a team lead or Scrum Master whose primary job during Day 1 team breakouts is to map cross-team dependencies in real time.
Tooling & Ecosystem
Jira Align / Rally: SAFe-native PI Planning boards with PI Objective tracking and dependency visualisation.
Miro / MURAL: virtual program board for distributed PI Planning — dependency arrows are drag-and-drop.
Confluence: PI Planning wiki with team PI Objectives, risk register, and capacity plan.
MLflow / Weights & Biases: model registry that serves as the source of truth for model version milestones on the program board.
Slack / Teams: dedicated PI channels (#pi4-announcements, #pi4-dependencies) created before the event to sustain coordination through the increment.
Zoom / Teams: breakout rooms for team planning sessions in virtual PI Planning.

Security & Reliability
PI Planning artefacts (PI Objectives with model quality targets, GPU capacity plans) are commercially sensitive — distribute via access-controlled channels.
The ROAM board may surface AI-specific risks relating to data privacy, model bias, or regulatory compliance — classify these appropriately and ensure only named stakeholders have access.

GPU capacity commitments made at PI Planning must be validated against cloud or on-premise quota before being committed as assumptions in PI Objectives — unvalidated capacity assumptions are a common plan failure.

Ensure that spike topics in the spike capacity register are reviewed for AI safety and ethics implications — a spike to 'explore more aggressive model compression' may have quality and safety implications that need governance visibility.
Key Takeaways
PI Planning is the most effective cross-team coordination mechanism for GenAI ARTs, resolving dependencies in 2 days that would take weeks asynchronously.
Feature slicing for AI — decomposing product features into data, model, infrastructure, and product components — is a mandatory pre-planning step that makes GenAI dependencies visible.
ML team PI Objectives must be framed as measurable model quality outcomes, not research activities: 'hallucination rate <3% on eval set' not 'improve model quality'.
Spike capacity (15-20% of team capacity) must be explicitly allocated and protected in the PI plan — it will otherwise be consumed by feature delivery pressure.
GPU capacity and data pipeline milestones must appear on the program board alongside product features — invisible AI infrastructure dependencies are the main mid-PI failure mode.
Confidence votes per PI Objective are particularly important for ML teams — model quality improvements with low confidence should be committed as uncommitted (stretch) objectives.
The model readiness assessment (current quality, training data status) is a mandatory pre-PI Planning artefact that prevents uninformed commitments.
